verses.commentary: Teaching from a boat. Crowds so large Jesus needs distance. The boat becomes a floating pulpit. Natural amphitheater by the lake.
He taught many things in parables (parabolē, παραβολή). Earthly stories with heavenly meaning. Indirect teaching. Parables conceal and reveal simultaneously.
Listen! (Akouete, Ἀκούετε). Imperative. Attention demanded. A farmer sows seed. Common scene, profound meaning.
Seed on the path. Hard ground. Birds devour it. No penetration. The word bounces off some hearts.
Rocky soil. Shallow earth. Quick germination. No depth. Surface response without substance.
Sun scorches the plant. No root means no resilience. Withers. Trials expose shallow faith.
Thorns choke the seed. Competition for resources. The word is planted but strangled. No fruit.
Good soil produces a crop. Thirty, sixty, a hundredfold. Exponential fruitfulness. The kingdom multiplies when it finds receptive hearts.
Whoever has ears to hear, let them hear. Repeated refrain. Hearing requires more than auditory function. Spiritual receptivity necessary.
The Twelve and others ask about the parables privately. Insiders seek understanding. Questions reveal hunger.
The secret (mystērion, μυστήριον) of the kingdom is given to you. Insider knowledge. Revelation is grace. But to those outside, everything is in parables. Outsiders remain confused. Parables divide.

Isaiah 6:9–10 quoted. Seeing but not perceiving, hearing but not understanding. Hardness of heart is judicial. Parables confirm pre-existing dispositions. Otherwise they might turn and be forgiven. The otherwise is key: God's mercy is available, but hardened hearts refuse it.
Don't you understand this parable? Rebuke to the disciples. How will you understand any parable? This is foundational. Master this, or remain in confusion.
The farmer sows the word. Simple key. The seed is the word (logos, λόγος). God's message, the gospel, the kingdom announcement.
Path = Satan steals the word immediately. Spiritual warfare. The enemy actively opposes the gospel. Hard hearts give him easy access.
Rocky soil = joyful reception. Immediate, emotional response. Looks promising.
No root, short-lived faith. Trouble or persecution exposes the shallowness. They fall away (skandalizontai, σκανδαλίζονται). Stumble, apostatize. Cost not counted.
Thorny soil = hear the word. Reception happens, but other things compete.

Worries of life, deceitfulness of wealth, desires for other things choke the word. Unfruitful. Not hostility but distraction. The tyranny of the urgent strangles the important. Wealth deceives (apatē, ἀπάτη); it promises what it cannot deliver.
Good soil = hear, accept, produce. Three verbs. Reception, internalization, manifestation. Fruit is inevitable when the word penetrates deeply. Varying yields, but all fruitful.
A lamp is not hidden under a bowl or bed. Absurd. Lamps illuminate. Purpose determines placement.
Nothing hidden that will not be disclosed. Present concealment serves future revelation. The kingdom is veiled now, manifest later. Parables are temporary pedagogy.
If anyone has ears to hear, let them hear. Repeated. Emphasis. Responsibility lies with the hearer.

Consider carefully what you hear. Quality of attention matters. With the measure you use, it will be measured to you, and even more. How you listen determines what you receive. Generous listening yields generous revelation.

Whoever has will be given more. Spiritual economics. Use brings increase. Whoever does not have, even what they have will be taken. Neglect leads to loss. Kingdom resources grow through engagement or atrophy through neglect.
Parable of the growing seed, unique to Mark. A man scatters seed. Active planting.

Night and day, the seed grows though he does not know how. Mystery of growth. Kingdom advancement is supernatural. Human effort does not produce it; God does. We plant and water, but God gives growth (1 Corinthians 3:6).
The soil produces grain automatically (automatē, αὐτομάτη). Organic, inevitable process. First stalk, then head, then full grain. Stages of maturity. Growth is gradual but certain.
When the grain is ripe, the sickle comes. Harvest. Joel 3:13 echoes. Eschatological imagery. The kingdom culminates in judgment and gathering.
What shall we say the kingdom is like? Jesus searches for analogies. The kingdom resists easy definition. Parables approximate.
Like a mustard seed, smallest of all seeds. Tiny, insignificant. The kingdom begins imperceptibly.

It becomes the largest garden plant. Birds nest in its branches. Echoes Ezekiel 17:23, Daniel 4:12. Small becomes great. Nations find refuge in the kingdom. Disproportionate growth from humble beginnings.
Jesus spoke in parables as much as they could understand. Pedagogical wisdom. Revelation calibrated to capacity. Not all at once, but progressively.
He did not speak without a parable publicly. But privately, he explained everything to the disciples. Two-tier teaching. Public parables, private explanation. Insiders receive more.
Evening comes. Let us go to the other side. Transition. The day of teaching ends; a night of revelation begins.
They take Jesus in the boat just as he was. Exhausted from teaching. Other boats follow. Witnesses to what comes next.
A furious squall. Sudden storm. The Sea of Galilee is known for violent weather. Waves break over the boat. Mortal danger. Experienced fishermen afraid.
Jesus sleeps on a cushion in the stern. Peace amid chaos. They wake him: Teacher, don't you care if we drown? Panic. Implied rebuke. They question his concern, not his power (not yet).

He rebukes the wind, commands the waves: Quiet! Be still! (Siōpa, pephimōso, Σιώπα, πεφίμωσο). Same language used for demons (1:25). He treats the storm as an adversary. Immediate calm. Perfect stillness.
Why are you so afraid? Do you still have no faith? Rebuke. Fear and faith are opposites. They should have trusted his presence and purpose. No faith despite all they have seen.

They are terrified. Bigger fear replaces smaller fear. Fear of the storm becomes fear of Jesus. Who is this? Even wind and waves obey him. The question of Jesus' identity intensifies. Only God commands creation (Psalm 89:9, 107:29). The chapter moves from seed parables to storm stilling. The word of the kingdom is sown; the Word of God calms chaos. Both demonstrate divine power.
